🟡 Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

HKY Intercultural Reserve is an Indigenous-led nonprofit initiative in Huni Kuin territory, dedicated to restoring Amazonian ecosystems while preserving and celebrating Huni Kuin culture. It aims to create sustainable livelihoods through agroforestry and cultural revitalization.

Bioregional Context

Situated in the Brazil-Peru border region of Acre, this area is part of the western Amazon and home to rich biocultural diversity. It faces threats from deforestation, extractive industries, and cultural erosion, with significant potential for regenerative Indigenous stewardship.

Place
AC, Brazil
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject|https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1VXzDAQLXPgoaNgo0Nzb0RlUfpjPUWOql69qagKkHKF0/edit?usp=sharing

Tags

🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🌰 Seeds_and_Nurseries 🎒 Learning_Programs 📖 Knowledge_Artefacts 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 📷 Visitor_Experiences

Background

Along the Brazil–Peru border in Acre, Huni Kuin communities have long stewarded Amazon forest. About 19,000 Huni Kuin people live across 12 reserves here, carrying shamanic practice, arts, and deep ecological knowledge—experience that matters for biocultural futures across the western Amazon.

Threats include illegal clearing, timber extraction, conventional agriculture, soil loss, and hunting pressure—forces that can displace communities from territory. At the same time, roughly 107,000 ha of unprotected land in the area could host regenerative models able to spread more widely in the basin.

Origins and Network

Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve forms around a dual aim: sustain Huni Kuin culture and spirituality while restoring forest ecosystems. Mapu Huni Kuin, spiritual leader and president of the Indigenous Association Yoshibu, is a visible advocate—linking political voice, ceremony, and defence of territory.

The mission is intergenerational self-sufficiency through viable enterprises rooted in Huni Kuin lifeways and adequate infrastructure—starting with wild cacao and wild pepper harvest and trade, strengthening identity, and applying forest coexistence knowledge to habitat recovery and cultural sovereignty.

Activities

🔸

Native Crop Regeneration and Supply Chain Development

 🔸

Youth Agroforestry and Cultural Training Program

 🔸

Native Tree Reforestation Campaign

 🔸

Sustainable Ethnobotanicals Research Program

 🔸

Huni Kuin Intercultural Exchange Hub

Vision and Legacy

The reserve is assembling a capital plan toward a $3.75 million, multi-tier allocation for restoration, enterprise integration, and the hub. Success is measured partly in replication: a model other Amazon forest communities might adapt—showing that agroforestry rooted in Huni Kuin practice can regenerate ecosystems and livelihoods together, with sovereignty and territory protection moving in parallel.
